description: Create a content slide titled The Implications with a left sidebar quote and a three-column text layout covering responsible AI, safety, trust, and business model implications of generalist robots.

header.kicker: Technology Vision 2025 | AI: A Declaration of Autonomy
header.headline: The Implications

sidebar.body: People’s acceptance of these machines will be critical to enterprises’ success as they reimagine how they operate and do business...

items.text: The emergence of generalist robots will steadily bring more AI autonomy into the physical world over the next decade. This new generation of robots will fuel a major reinvention of enterprises’ physical presences, redesign operations, power new services, and engage people and the world differently. Enterprises need to prepare now and build a trusted environment for these machines to flourish.

Enterprises will need to extend responsible AI practices into the real world. Robotics raises new questions around data privacy, such as robots continuously collecting audio and visual information in public spaces, compliance with privacy and consent laws, and tradeoffs between local data storage and the ability for machines to learn.

There will also be physical safety and governance challenges. Beyond hacking or malfunction, robots may face situations where harm is unavoidable and will need to make choices about how to minimize it. Enterprises should plan now for the decision-making frameworks these environments require, using responsible AI as a baseline for building trust.

Enterprises also need confidence in the investment because this generation of robots may require fundamentally different business models. Leaders should explore how they will access these machines, how robotics fits into cost structures, and how those choices affect use cases and partner strategies.

Generalist robots’ multipurpose capabilities make robotics-as-a-service an attractive model. Renting adaptable machines can lower upfront hardware investment, reduce risk in testing new use cases, and allow redeployment or return of robots. Enterprises should evaluate value versus risks, including questions about data collection, proprietary business knowledge, and what can be shared with providers.
